text:
HEMATOLOGY/BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT CLINIC NOTE — TRANSPLANT EVALUATION COMPLETION / HLA RESULTS REVIEW

ATTENDING: Hematology/BMT Attending
SERVICE: Bone Marrow Transplantation
VISIT TYPE: Established outpatient — Transplant evaluation follow-up
Note: Approximately 8 weeks from initial AML diagnosis; this represents the formal BMT program evaluation completion visit.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Renata Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old female with newly diagnosed, de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M4 subtype, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN 2022 Adverse risk, who presents today for review of HLA typing results, donor search update, and completion of pre-transplant evaluation in preparation for planned allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first complete remission (CR1).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old high school biology teacher who presented approximately 8 weeks ago with a 3-week history of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, spontaneous gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and drenching night sweats. She was found to have marked leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/mcL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL), and thrombocytopenia (platelets 34,000/mcL). Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML, FAB M4, with 74% blasts and circulating blasts with Auer rods on peripheral smear. Comprehensive molecular workup by 523-gene NGS panel identified FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.82 (VAF 82%, insertion length 48 bp) and DNMT3A R882H co-mutation (VAF 47%). NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, CEBPA, TP53, RUNX1, and all other prognostically relevant genes were wild-type. Cytogenetics showed a normal female karyotype (46,XX). She was classified as ELN 2022 Adverse risk based on FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in the absence of NPM1 mutation.

She was admitted and received standard induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen (cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1–7; daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 2, and 3) plus midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8–21, per the RATIFY trial protocol for FLT3-mutant AML. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (pan-sensitive, treated with narrowed antibiotics after cultures), Grade 3 mucositis requiring IV morphine and parenteral nutrition for several days, and Grade 2 nausea/vomiting. Neutropenia lasted 22 days. Midostaurin was held briefly during peak mucositis and then resumed. She recovered from all acute toxicities prior to the day 28 marrow assessment.

Day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete morphologic remission (CR1): blast count 2%, no morphologically aberrant blasts, MRD-negative by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%), and FLT3-ITD undetectable by PCR (sensitivity 10^-4). DNMT3A R882H persisted at VAF 1.8% by ddPCR, felt to represent pre-existing clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) rather than residual disease given the MRD-negative status by flow and FLT3-ITD PCR, and the known association of DNMT3A R882H with CHIP. Cytogenetics restored to 46,XX.

At her day 35 clinic visit, the decision was made to pursue allogeneic transplant in CR1 given her adverse-risk disease features. This recommendation is strongly supported by European LeukemiaNet guidance and multiple large retrospective and prospective analyses demonstrating that alloHSCT in CR1 provides a substantial relapse-free and overall survival benefit for patients with FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio AML compared to chemotherapy alone, even with midostaurin maintenance. HLA typing was sent for the patient and her two siblings at that visit, and an unrelated donor search was initiated simultaneously through NMDP/Be The Match as a contingency, given the estimated 25% per-sibling probability of HLA match.

Subsequently, a PET-CT scan was performed as part of transplant workup. This showed resolution of the splenomegaly (spleen now 11.8 cm, down from 14.2 cm at diagnosis), no FDG-avid foci to suggest extramedullary leukemia or granulocytic sarcoma, and mild residual bilateral iliac crest hypermetabolism consistent with ongoing marrow recovery. The metabolic imaging was reassuring and cleared her for proceeding with transplant from an extramedullary disease standpoint.

Today we are reviewing the complete HLA typing results and finalizing the transplant plan.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Acute myeloid leukemia, FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 Adverse risk — currently in CR1, MRD-negative
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — managed with metformin; currently held during active treatment period
3. Mild hypertension — previously managed, currently clinically stable without pharmacologic intervention
4. No prior hematologic conditions; de novo AML (no antecedent MDS or prior cytotoxic exposures)

SURGICAL HISTORY
- PICC line placement (day 0 of induction) — currently in place, no complications
- Bone marrow biopsy x2 (diagnostic and day +28 response assessment)

FAMILY HISTORY
- No family history of leukemia, myelodysplastic syndrome, lymphoma, or other hematologic malignancies
- No known familial cancer syndromes
- Two siblings alive and well (one in their 50s, one in their early 40s)

SOCIAL HISTORY
- High school biology teacher; currently on medical leave from work since diagnosis
- Married; lives with husband and two adult children, who have been actively supportive throughout the treatment course
- Non-smoker; no history of tobacco, alcohol excess, or illicit drug use
- No significant occupational exposures relevant to secondary AML etiology
- Motivated, health-literate patient who has done extensive personal research on her diagnosis and transplant options

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies
- No known food or environmental allergies

MEDICATIONS (current)
1. Midostaurin (Rydapt) 50 mg orally twice daily — FLT3 inhibitor, currently in post-cycle maintenance window; will be held during bridging consolidation cytotoxic phase and resumed on days 8–21 of consolidation
2. Levofloxacin 500 mg orally daily — antibacterial prophylaxis (to be continued until start of conditioning)
3. Fluconazole 400 mg orally daily — antifungal prophylaxis
4. Acyclovir 400 mg orally twice daily — antiviral prophylaxis (HSV/VZV)
5. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS one tablet orally three times weekly — PCP prophylaxis
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed — antiemetic
7. Metformin — currently held due to treatment; plan to resume post-transplant once renal function confirmed stable
8. Calcium carbonate 500 mg + Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally twice daily — initiated prophylactically
9. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue improving; no fever, no night sweats currently. Weight has partially recovered (64 kg, down from baseline 67 kg; ~4.5% weight loss).
HEENT: No gingival bleeding (completely resolved). No oral ulcers currently. Mild residual xerostomia from prior mucositis, improving.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea at rest. Exertional dyspnea has resolved with anemia correction.
Pulmonary: No cough, no shortness of breath on exertion.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite returning; tolerating regular diet. No nausea at present. No diarrhea. Bowel function normal.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria. Menstrual cycles have not resumed since initiating chemotherapy (expected chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea; reproductive endocrine implications noted and to be discussed at length).
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning and proximal muscle fatigue; no bone pain.
Neurologic: No peripheral neuropathy symptoms currently. No focal neurologic deficits.
Skin: No rash.
Psychiatric: Patient acknowledges significant anxiety about the transplant process and long-term outcomes. She has been engaging with the hospital social worker and has found this helpful. She remains determined and optimistic.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 82 bpm (regular), temperature 36.7°C, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 64.2 kg. BMI 23.8 kg/m2. ECOG PS: 0 (fully active, able to carry on all pre-disease activities).

General: Well-appearing, alert, and oriented female in no acute distress. Affect is somewhat anxious but engaged. Conversant and articulate.

HEENT: Sclerae clear, no icterus. Oral mucosa moist and intact; no evidence of active mucositis, ulceration, or residual gingival bleeding. Dentition intact. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge not palpable below the costal margin. Spleen not palpable (consistent with resolution of prior splenomegaly on recent PET-CT). No masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No ecchymoses or petechiae currently. PICC line site on right upper extremity is clean, dry, and without signs of infection or phlebitis.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.

Skin: No rash, petechiae, purpura, or jaundice.

Lymph nodes: No palpable adenopathy in axillary, inguinal, or epitrochlear chains.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today's visit)
CBC: WBC 4,600/mcL; ANC 3,200/mcL; Hgb 11.2 g/dL; MCV 88 fL; Platelets 121,000/mcL
Differential: Neutrophils 69%, lymphocytes 22%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 1%, no blasts identified on manual differential
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.88 mg/dL (eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2), glucose 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated in context of recent steroid use and stress), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, albumin 3.9 g/dL, total protein 7.1 g/dL
LDH: 162 U/L (normal)
Uric acid: 4.8 mg/dL (normal)
Coagulation: PT 12.1 sec, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec (normal)
CMV IgG serology (patient): Positive (confirmed at diagnosis)
EBV IgG (patient): Positive
HSV IgG (patient): Positive
HBsAg: Negative; HBs Ab: Positive (prior vaccination); HBc Ab: Negative
HCV Ab: Negative
HIV Ag/Ab: Negative
Tacrolimus trough: Not applicable (not yet on tacrolimus)
HbA1c (from last month): 7.4% (adequate diabetes control given current metabolic stress)

---

HLA TYPING RESULTS (reviewed today)
PATIENT HLA (high-resolution 10/10 allele-level typing, NGS method):
  HLA-A: A*02:01, A*24:02
  HLA-B: B*07:02, B*35:01
  HLA-C: C*07:02, C*04:01
  HLA-DRB1: DRB1*11:01, DRB1*15:01
  HLA-DQB1: DQB1*03:01, DQB1*06:02

SIBLING HLA RESULTS:
  Sibling 1 (older sister, age 54): HLA-B locus mismatch confirmed — 7/10 allele match at high resolution. NOT a suitable matched related donor (MRD).
  Sibling 2 (younger brother, age 43): HLA-C and HLA-DRB1 mismatches confirmed — 7/10 allele match at high resolution. NOT a suitable matched related donor (MRD).

Unrelated Donor Search (NMDP/Be The Match): An unrelated donor was identified. Donor is a 38-year-old male, CMV seronegative (CMV IgG negative). High-resolution confirmatory typing confirms 10/10 HLA allele-level match (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1). Donor has been contacted and has confirmed willingness to donate. Donor workup in progress.

DONOR/RECIPIENT CMV PAIRING: Recipient CMV IgG positive / Donor CMV IgG negative. This D-/R+ pairing carries an intermediate-to-higher risk of CMV reactivation post-transplant, which will necessitate weekly CMV surveillance by quantitative PCR from approximately day +14 through at least day +100, with pre-emptive valganciclovir therapy if reactivation is detected. Discussed with patient.

DONOR STEM CELL SOURCE SELECTED: Peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC), mobilized with G-CSF. PBSC is preferred over bone marrow in this MUD adult alloHSCT setting for faster engraftment kinetics, though it is associated with a somewhat higher rate of chronic GVHD compared to bone marrow. The risk-benefit balance was discussed with the patient, and given the priority of engraftment reliability and faster count recovery in this high-risk AML patient, PBSC was selected.

---

PRE-TRANSPLANT EVALUATION SUMMARY
All required pre-transplant assessments have now been completed:

1. Cardiac evaluation: Transthoracic echocardiogram performed at diagnosis — LVEF 62%, normal biventricular function, no valvular disease. No cardiac contraindication to myeloablative conditioning. CLEARED.

2. Pulmonary function tests (performed this week): FEV1 88% predicted; FVC 91% predicted; FEV1/FVC ratio 0.80; DLCO 82% predicted. No significant obstructive or restrictive ventilatory defect. Adequate pulmonary reserve for FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning. CLEARED.

3. Dental evaluation: Complete dental examination performed; no active dental infections or dental procedures outstanding. CLEARED.

4. Ophthalmology clearance: Baseline ophthalmologic examination completed; no contraindications identified for planned conditioning. CLEARED.

5. Renal function: eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m2 today (stable; no significant renal impairment). CLEARED.

6. Hepatic function: LFTs normal; no hepatic contraindication identified. CLEARED.

7. Infectious disease screen: HIV negative, HBsAg negative, HCV negative. CMV IgG positive (recipient). CLEARED with CMV monitoring plan in place.

8. Disease status: CR1 confirmed by day 28 bone marrow biopsy and aspirate; MRD negative by multiparameter flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR. PET-CT negative for extramedullary AML. CLEARED — disease status adequate for proceeding with transplant.

9. Performance status: ECOG PS 0. Fully functional. CLEARED.

10. Psychosocial assessment: Patient has been evaluated by transplant social work. She has a strong support system (husband, two adult children). No major psychosocial barriers identified. She demonstrates good health literacy and a clear understanding of the transplant process and its risks. Patient-stated goals: curative intent. CLEARED.

11. BMT program acceptance: Formal transplant program case review completed. Patient accepted for myeloablative allogeneic HSCT from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) in CR1. Transplant date targeted for approximately 4 weeks from today, pending completion of donor workup and stem cell collection.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed)
PET-CT (performed approximately 1 week ago): No FDG-avid foci consistent with extramedullary leukemia or granulocytic sarcoma. Splenomegaly has resolved (spleen 11.8 cm). Mild bilateral iliac crest hypermetabolism consistent with bone marrow recovery post-induction. No lymphadenopathy. This is reassuring and confirms no evidence of active extramedullary disease prior to transplant.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Kowalczyk is a 48-year-old woman with de novo AML, FAB M4, harboring FLT3-ITD at a high allelic ratio (AR 0.82) and DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 Adverse-risk classification, who has achieved complete morphologic and molecular remission (CR1, MRD-negative) after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin induction. She has now completed full pre-transplant evaluation and a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor has been identified. She is cleared for proceeding with allogeneic HSCT.

1. ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION — PLANNED IN CR1:
   - INDICATION: Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high AR, NPM1 wild-type) in CR1, MRD-negative. This is a Category 1 indication for alloHSCT in CR1 per ELN 2022, NCCN, and published randomized data from the AMLSG and MRC collaborative groups. Without transplant, the relapse risk for this disease biology at 2 years is estimated at 60–70% with chemotherapy alone, even with midostaurin maintenance.
   - DONOR: 10/10 HLA allele-level matched unrelated donor (MUD), 38-year-old male, CMV negative. This is the best available alternative to a matched sibling donor and outcomes with a 10/10 MUD are comparable to matched related donor transplants in this disease setting.
   - STEM CELL SOURCE: Peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC)
   - CONDITIONING REGIMEN PLANNED: Myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) — Fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day x 4 days (days -6 to -3) + Busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV x 4 days (days -6 to -3). PK-guided busulfan dosing to target AUC 4500–6000 micromol*min/L will be employed. Rationale: FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning is selected given patient's young age (48), excellent performance status, normal organ function, and the need for maximum anti-leukemic intensity in this high-risk, aggressive disease. The myeloablative approach provides a more potent graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect compared to reduced-intensity conditioning, which is critical for this FLT3-ITD high-risk case.
   - GVHD PROPHYLAXIS: Tacrolimus/Trexall combination. Tacrolimus IV initiated on day -3, targeting a trough level of 8–12 ng/mL. Short-course Trexall 15 mg/m2 IV on day +1, 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, and +11. Additionally, rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG, Thymoglobulin) 2.5 mg/kg/day x 3 days (days -3, -2, -1; total 7.5 mg/kg) will be administered for additional GVHD prophylaxis and to facilitate engraftment in the MUD setting, per institutional protocol.
   - Discussed with patient the risks and benefits of the transplant procedure in detail, including:
     a. Transplant-related mortality (TRM) at 1 year estimated 5–10% for a patient of this profile (young, good PS, MUD 10/10, first CR)
     b. Acute GVHD risk (Grade II–IV: ~25–35% with tacrolimus/MTX/ATG; Grade III–IV: ~10–15%)
     c. Chronic GVHD risk (~40–50% with PBSC source; though most cases are mild-moderate and manageable)
     d. Infectious risks during the immunocompromised period (bacterial, fungal, viral including CMV — noted above; PCP)
     e. SOS/VOD risk with busulfan-based conditioning (use of PK dosing mitigates this risk)
     f. Pulmonary complications including diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, idiopathic pneumonia syndrome
     g. Long-term effects: gonadal failure and premature ovarian insufficiency (highly likely given conditioning; patient already has chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea; reproductive endocrinology referral placed today for counseling and fertility discussion, recognizing that fertility preservation before transplant is not feasible at this timeline given urgency; hormone replacement therapy options post-transplant will be discussed)
     h. Secondary malignancy risk (very low in the short-term but not zero at 10–20 years; skin cancers and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder are rare known late risks)
     i. Tacrolimus nephrotoxicity with prolonged use
   - Patient and her husband attended this discussion. Patient confirms understanding and informed consent obtained. Patient wishes to proceed.

2. BRIDGING CONSOLIDATION PRIOR TO TRANSPLANT:
   Given the approximately 4-week interval until the transplant date (required for donor mobilization and travel logistics), we plan to administer one cycle of bridging intermediate-dose cytarabine (IDAC) consolidation to maintain remission and reduce risk of relapse during the pre-transplant window. This is standard practice at our institution for patients awaiting MUD transplant. Specific regimen to be discussed at next visit; cytarabine 1.5 g/m2 IV every 12 hours on days 1, 3, and 5 is planned. Midostaurin will be continued on days 8–21 per protocol. Patient is aware that another hospitalization will be required for this consolidation cycle and has agreed.

3. CMV MANAGEMENT PLAN:
   - Patient is CMV IgG positive (seropositive recipient); donor is CMV IgG negative. This D-/R+ pairing is the highest-risk CMV serostatus combination.
   - Plan: Weekly quantitative CMV PCR from blood starting at approximately day +14 post-transplant, continuing through at least day +100, and longer if on immunosuppression beyond that point.
   - Pre-emptive therapy approach: If CMV viremia is detected at any level, will initiate pre-emptive valganciclovir (or IV ganciclovir if oral absorption is a concern during mucositis). Letermovir prophylaxis will be considered per transplant protocol beginning day +1.
   - Discussed with patient.

4. REPRODUCTIVE AND ENDOCRINE CONSIDERATIONS:
   - Patient has had chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea since induction. Myeloablative conditioning with busulfan will almost certainly result in permanent premature ovarian insufficiency (POI).
   - Reproductive endocrinology consultation placed today. While fertility preservation (embryo/oocyte cryopreservation) is not feasible in the current urgent clinical timeline, we discussed the importance of hormone replacement therapy post-transplant to protect bone density, cardiovascular health, cognitive function, and quality of life.
   - Patient was emotionally affected by this discussion but appreciates the transparent communication. She and her husband will follow up with reproductive endocrinology prior to transplant admission.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
   - Metformin remains held during active treatment. HbA1c 7.4% — acceptable in the current context.
   - Will discuss with primary care regarding resumption of metformin post-transplant once renal function is confirmed stable (metformin hold appropriate given anticipated transient renal impairment in the peri-transplant period and with tacrolimus-based immunosuppression).
   - Note: Tacrolimus has known diabetogenic effects (inhibits insulin secretion); will monitor glucose closely post-transplant and anticipate need for insulin or adjustment of diabetes management strategy.

6. PICC LINE:
   - Current PICC line will be removed prior to conditioning and replaced with a tunneled central venous catheter (double or triple lumen Hickman catheter) at the time of transplant admission, to facilitate long-term access for conditioning regimen, blood products, and prolonged IV medications during the transplant period.

7. INFECTIOUS PROPHYLAXIS PLAN (pre-transplant — continuing current regimen):
   - Levofloxacin, fluconazole, acyclovir, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP prophylaxis): Continue through transplant admission; prophylaxis regimen will be transitioned to transplant-specific protocol at time of admission.

8. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Return to clinic in 1 week for bridging consolidation planning and initiation of pre-admission transplant preparations.
   - Transplant nurse coordinator will contact patient within 48 hours with specific admission and logistics information.
   - Patient instructed to call with any fever (temperature ≥38.0°C), new bleeding, or other concerning symptoms.

---

Time spent: 75 minutes (30 minutes face-to-face with patient and family, 45 minutes reviewing records, HLA results, donor search data, completing documentation, and coordination with transplant team).

Electronically signed by: Attending Physician, Hematology/Bone Marrow Transplant Program
